Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I'm 27, so I've got a lot of life experience figuring out what works for me. I'd typically listen to lectures and audio materials during my commute or while doing chores, since I learn best through listening. I'd block out maybe three to four hours on certain days for deeper study, but I rarely followed a strict schedule. The gamified tasks actually helped me stay somewhat on track because they gave me small goals to hit.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. With only 24% attendance, I clearly wasn't showing up regularly to live sessions, but that doesn't mean I wasn't working. Deadlines definitely drove my more intense study periods. Some weeks I'd put in serious hours, other weeks barely anything visible. I found myself cramming discussions and assignments into specific windows rather than spreading them evenly.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check on deadlines or when I got a notification about something new. Sometimes the gamified elements pulled me in because I wanted to see my progress or unlock the next challenge. I'd also log in if I knew there was a discussion I needed to contribute to, since those peer interactions were actually pretty valuable. But I'll be honest, I didn't log in just to browse around—I needed a specific reason.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became habitual, especially when the gamified tasks were active and I was on a streak. That sense of progression made me want to maintain momentum. But it never fully stuck as a daily routine for me. Life would interrupt, my anxiety would spike about something else, and suddenly a week had passed without logging in. The routine was fragile.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful—I watched over 190 minutes worth because I could replay sections and really absorb the content auditorily. Discussion forums were also valuable because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click for me. The gamified tasks felt rewarding because they broke things into manageable chunks. When I could listen to something multiple times at my own pace, that's when real learning happened for me.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources. My learning style is auditory, so walls of text just don't stick for me the same way. I'd skim them at best or skip them entirely if there was an audio alternative. Some of the optional quizzes I delayed because my anxiety made me avoid things that felt like tests, even low-stakes ones. I know I left resources on the table.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams, or when it had that gamified element that made progress tangible. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. The discussion posts felt worthwhile because the peer interactions actually deepened my understanding. Anything that felt like busywork without clear payoff got deprioritized quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they carried less weight emotionally, which actually made them easier to approach sometimes. Required activities triggered more of my anxiety because the stakes felt real. But paradoxically, I'd sometimes engage more genuinely with optional content because there was no pressure. The gamified optional tasks hit a sweet spot—they felt like choices but still gave me rewards and structure.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded audio files and lectures constantly so I could study offline during commutes or while exercising. I'd take voice memos of my own thoughts and summaries, then listen back to them. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos on related topics. A lot of my actual learning happened with headphones in, completely disconnected from the LMS.
Q10: Most of my studying looked invisible in the system. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it three or four times over the following days—none of that repeat engagement showed up. My voice memo study sessions, my conversations with classmates outside the platform, my time processing ideas while walking—none of that registers. The LMS only saw the tip of the iceberg.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or audio file once, download it, and then that material would live on my phone for weeks. I'd revisit it multiple times offline. Same with discussion threads—I'd screenshot interesting points and review them later. One download could represent hours of actual study time that never appeared in the logs.
Q12: My anxiety really spiked at certain points, and that made everything harder. Despite having high resilience, there were weeks where just opening the LMS felt overwhelming. When multiple deadlines converged or when I felt behind, I'd freeze up rather than push through. My low motivation baseline meant I needed external structure to keep going, and when that structure felt chaotic, I'd disengage.
Q13: Definitely. Competing responsibilities as a 27-year-old meant I couldn't always prioritize coursework the way younger students might. Fatigue hit hard some weeks, and my anxiety made even simple tasks feel monumental. When I was confused about expectations, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely rather than face that uncertainty. My task completion rate of 56% reflects those struggles pretty clearly.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but my mind was elsewhere—anxious about other things or just mentally exhausted. Those sessions generated activity data but zero actual learning. It was like going through the motions to feel productive without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. My anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes—the fear of doing it wrong paralyzed me. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow came with its own challenges. The irony is my assignment scores were strong when I actually completed things, but getting to that point was the battle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator given my low intrinsic motivation. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become intensely focused, cramming engagement into short windows. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines throughout. Without external time pressure, I'd drift. My LMS activity probably looked very spiky around due dates.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more likely to engage again. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, could trigger my anxiety and make me avoid that area for a while. I needed to process feedback emotionally before I could act on it. The delay in my response time probably reflects that processing period.
Q18: The course structure really mattered for someone like me with low motivation. When things were clearly laid out with obvious next steps, I could follow along. When the structure was ambiguous, my anxiety filled in the gaps with worst-case scenarios. The gamified elements provided structure I desperately needed. But the overall flexibility that probably helped others sometimes left me adrift.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they gave me clear goals and rewards that my anxious brain could latch onto. The audio and video content suited my learning style perfectly. What made it harder was when important information was buried in text documents or when expectations weren't crystal clear. Ambiguity is my enemy when anxiety is high.
Q20: I think that pattern reflects students who are anxious and trying to engage but struggling to produce. They might be like me—opening materials repeatedly, starting and stopping, gathering information but freezing when it comes to output. High activity can mask paralysis. The LMS sees the clicking around but not the internal struggle to actually complete something.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was decent and my assignment quality was strong when I submitted, but my LMS activity was pretty minimal in terms of logins. I learned a lot offline through audio materials and peer conversations. Someone could look at my login frequency and think I was disengaged, but my actual learning happened elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, surface-level engagement trends. But they completely miss the quality of attention, offline study, emotional state, and actual comprehension. They can't capture me listening to a downloaded lecture for the fifth time or having a breakthrough during a walk. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, connecting it to things I know, and feeling some investment in understanding it. It's not about time spent or clicks made—it's about mental presence. For me, engagement often happens through listening and reflecting, which might look like inactivity. True engagement is when the material occupies space in my mind beyond the study session.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels and how they fluctuated throughout the course. It wouldn't capture my extensive offline audio-based studying or the peer conversations that clarified concepts for me. It can't see that my low attendance and moderate engagement masked genuine learning happening in unconventional ways. My Merit grade came from real understanding, just acquired differently than the data would suggest.
